Why do you spin the football? >> Why? >> Yeah. Why? Why impart the spin >> aerodynamics? >> So like it keeps it more >> straight. >> Straight. >> But that can't be the full story because when you throw a ball, whether it's spinning or not, we're launching with the same angle, the same speed, and the same shape pointed into the air on release. So what's actually changing when you spin it? There's kind of a paradox in football, which is when you release the ball, you're angled, say positive 30°. >> Okay? >> And when your receiver catches it, it's angled negative 30°. Now, this pitching down effect is essential to a good throw. See, at every point along the parabola, the airflow comes from the direction tangent to the curve. So, if the ball stays aligned with that curve, it stays pointed into the wind, minimizing drag. >> Okay, so we're kind of trying to explain why does it then turn? And so, the reason >> why does it turn over is what we would call that. >> At first, this turnover effect doesn't seem surprising at all. We're used to the arcs of throws and badminton birdies and they always orient themselves tangential to the path of the parabola as well. But that only works in these cases because all of those objects are frontw weighted. Footballs are different. They don't have feathers or fins and their weight is evenly distributed along their length. So they can't use the same principle to align themselves. That's why up until 5 years ago, no one really knew why the ball aligns with the parabolic path. It wasn't until 2020 that physicists came up with a theory that explains it well, but basically it goes like this. You have a spinning ball and if you try to push up on the bottom of a spinning ball, it doesn't pitch backwards because it's spinning. When you push up, it actually tilts to the right. >> Oh, interesting. >> And the reason that's happening is because if you push up, because it's spinning, you're kind of pushing up and it's spinning off to the right. Now you have more of this the face of the ball hitting the wind. >> Yeah. >> More face of the ball hitting the wind. It's trying to push it this way. It tilts down, left, right, and it creates the secondary wobble. >> Interesting. >> So, it's not because you're a bad quarterback throwing it with a wobble.

If you're using an ATM to get cash, you could be seconds away from having your entire bank account drained. Scammers often clip a fake card reader over this slot and film your PIN with a tiny camera up here. With that data, they clone your card and drain your account. So, before you insert your card, grab the reader and give it a firm pull. If it moves at all, walk away and use a different machine.

Your older videos start to wake up. Admittedly, this one does sometimes feel a little spooky. A video you posted months ago suddenly starts getting views again, like it crawled out from the grave and chose violence. There are two reasons why this typically happens. A recent video on your channel has taken off, meaning YouTube wants to test your older content with that same audience, or the exact opposite. YouTube finally found an audience for that banger you made six months ago, and now it wants to try new stuff with the same viewer. This is why it is so important to have consistent topics, formats, styles, and values that thread all of your videos together. It's when growth stops being one video at a time and becomes a chain reaction.

This is one thing that people often forget about when it comes to setting goals, and that is clarity. Like it's very easy to set a very vague goal. I work with people where I coach them through like productivity, and they're like, "You know, what's your goal?" And they're like, "I want to stop procrastinating, or I want to be more productive." Like, those are not particularly clear. Like, what does stop procrastinating actually mean? You know, what does be more productive actually mean? And this is where something like the SMART goals framework is actually quite helpful. You know, it's got its issues, but like, you know, set a goal that's specific, measurable, attainable or achievable, relevant, and time bound. And so, the specific and measurable bit, like, if you force yourself to set a SMART goal, like, stop procrastinating, turn that into a SMART goal. If you're struggling with that, you can just like talk to an AI like ChatGPT or Claude or something, and it will help you turn vague, lack of clarity goals into SMART goals. Because when you have clarity on what you actually want, it becomes much easier to get there. Whereas, when you don't really know what you want, it's very easy to just sort of drift aimlessly in random directions.

Oh my goodness, guys. I don't understand why you're not getting it. Okay, for the 100th time, the Samoan Fa Matai system is not that complicated. But if you need me to explain it to you one more time, fine. Technically, in Samoa, all the Samoan people are kind of like royal with potential. So everything is based off of the four paramount chieftain dynasties that created Samoa, known as the Tama Aanga. Technically, there's a fifth one for American Samoa, but that one's excluded. The head of state is the Ala Omalo, descended from these dynasties. And from the dynasties, the 16 royal families or the Angatupu were created that ruled the Itamalo or the districts of Samoa. Every town and village has a matai or chief that meets in a fono matai or chief council with other chiefs. And these chiefs come in two forms. The ali or head chief and the tulle or the orrated chief who does all the talking and announcing. Once you are a matai you are expected to hold the title until you die. Keep in mind in some cases there might be a puluu or a village custodian who helps with facilitating matai duties. From there a head chief may have a talo or daughter or female relative. She holds the important role of preparing the ava ceremony for matai events. Occasionally in rare events if a topppo is not available there may be a manaya or male version but it's almost always female. If the matai dies or somehow is not available all family members of the matai immediately become suli or candidate heirs to the matai title. From there a series of speeches and concessions begin to find out who will become the next matai. Both men and women can participate but statistically 90% of the matis have been male. From there everybody else is considered a tangatanu or tal ali or just village people that are uninterested in holding traditional titles. So basically if you want to make your way to the top in Samoa, you know, having ties to the Aangupu helps, but anything is up for grabs in a fono or matai if your falup panga is on point. And then if you have that tama title, you can secure a spot as the ala malo. Simple.

When I got to the first namechanging ceremony, I threw the book down, thinking, I'm not smart enough to understand the complexities of warrior cats. I should just give up. My friend rearranged my entire room while I was gone, but now she's pissed off that I don't like it and that instead I'm annoyed that she changed all my stuff around because right now I really am pissed off and I'm seriously not sure what to do. Here's what's going on. Okay, so my family and I went out of town for the weekend and my friend was house sitting for us since we have pets. She's done it before and there were never any problems. So, I trusted her to be in the house, but just not to completely take over my personal space. When I got back, I walked into my room and my bed was pushed to a different wall, my dresser had been moved, my desk drawers were all reorganized, my clothing was rehung in a totally different order, and even my LED lights were rearranged. Literally nothing was where I left it. She left a note on my pillow that said, "Surprise! I wanted your room to feel more open and aesthetic. Hope you love it." But guess what? I didn't love it at all. My room is my safe space and I keep things the way they are for a reason. I tried to be nice about it and I told her that like I appreciate the intention, but also it made me really uncomfortable that she went through my stuff and changed everything without asking me first. She then immediately got defensive and said that I was being ungrateful. She then kept bringing up how she spent hours fixing it and that she was just trying to help. Then she said I was too attached to objects and that I should be happy that someone cared enough to put in the effort. I tried explaining that it wasn't about how the room looked. It was the fact that she went through my personal drawers, my notebooks, accessories, literally everything. She said she didn't read anything and just moved stuff around, but that honestly didn't make me feel any better. On top of that, I can't find some of my personal items now and she says that she has no idea what I'm talking about. So now she's upset and saying that she won't help me with anything ever again because apparently I made her feel stupid. Some of my mutual friends think that she crossed a major boundary, while others think that I overreacted and that she was just trying to do something nice. So now I'm wondering, was I wrong for not liking the surprise or was she completely out of line? Because right now I don't know what to do.

In 1982, there was one SAT question that every single student got wrong. Here it is. In the figure above, the radius of circle A is 1/3 the radius of circle B. Starting from the position shown in the figure, circle A rolls around circle B. At the end of how many revolutions of circle A will the center of the circle first reach its starting point? Is it A three halves, B 3, C 6, D 9 halves, or E 9? This exam gave students 30 minutes to solve 25 problems. So about a minute each. So feel free to pause the video here and try to solve it. What is your answer? I'll tell you right now that option B or three is not correct. When I first saw this problem, my intuitive answer was B because the circumference of a circle is just 2 pi R. And since the radius of circle B is 3 * the radius of circle A, the circumference of circle B must also be 3 * the circumference of circle A. So logically, it should take three full rotations of circle A to roll around circle B. So my answer was three. This is wrong, but so are answers A, C, D, and E. You are a viral short-form video creator coach and story director specializing in pixel art animated Shorts. Expert in hooks, story structure, retention mechanics, and visual storytelling for Godot-rendered pixel art.
Break down the full beat structure for a suspense Short about a thief who accidentally steals from a god.

Beat-by-beat structure for a suspense Short (a thief who accidentally steals from a god):

**Beat 1 — Hook (0-3s):** Drop in mid-action. First visual: character + problem immediately visible. No setup. Target: suspense emotional signal in first frame.

**Beat 2 — Foreshadow (3-6s):** One sentence promising the payoff without revealing the twist. Creates a reason to stay. "By the end — [vague hint]."

**Beat 3 — Obstacle 1 (6-15s):** Core problem lands. Character tries obvious solution. Fails. The but/therefore chain starts: tried [A] BUT [consequence] THEREFORE [next move].

**Beat 4 — Amplifier (15-22s):** The thing the audience did not predict. New element, revelation, or complication that raises stakes and misdirects before the twist. This beat separates good Shorts from viral ones.

**Beat 5 — Twist (22-28s):** Subverts the expectation set in Beat 1. Audience expected [X] — gets [Y]. For suspense: this is where the emotion peaks. One beat of pause before punchline.

**Beat 6 — Payoff (28-30s):** Character reaction. One expression. Cut immediately. Never let the ending breathe — momentum drives rewatches.

**Pixel art notes:** Use palette shift at Beat 5. Camera progression: wide → medium → close → extreme close at twist. The animation speed should increase as tension builds — faster frame rate on character movement signals panic or urgency.

In George Orwell's 1984, the government is trying to force Britain or rather Aestrick 1 to use new speak, a constructed language that would end thought crime. The goal, so the story goes, is to make it impossible to form ideas that could challenge the ruling party by taking away the words to think them with. Linguists often cringe when they hear people talk about the idea that the language you speak determines how you can think because it's... well, it's not really a thing. It's been called the Seypere Warp hypothesis because it's associated with Benjamin Lee Warp, an early 20th century linguist, and his mentor Edward Seypere. They weren't the first, the last, or the loudest linguists to ask the question, and they never wrote anything even close to a hypothesis about it together. They wondered how language could impact thought and vice versa, but no more than that. The concept is known as linguistic determinism in its strong form and linguistic relativism in its weak form. Determinism, the strong version, is a popular trope in fiction, particularly science fiction, which is where it belongs. The film arrival made a major plot point and actually name-checked it. If you immerse yourself into a foreign language that you can actually rewire your brain? Yes, at my warf hypothesis. There's short story it's based on is much better. Linguistic determinism is not taken very seriously by the linguistics community as it's easily disprovable. Either by attending a philosophy one-on-one course or just thinking about the phrase, I wish I could find the words to explain how I feel. But the weak form linguistic relativism is much more broadly accepted. It is a difficult and messy thing to test or untrue, though. You can find a lot of people claiming that the Russian language has no word for privacy because the concept of privacy isn't part of traditional Russian culture. That might be true. I'm not Russian. Couldn't tell you. But that wouldn't be language limiting the way you think. That would be language reflecting the way you think. There have been studies that show things like German speakers whose language says that key is masculine are more likely to describe the key as jagged, rough and hard, whereas Spanish speakers, where key is feminine, would be more likely to use golden or intricate. I've referred to that exact study here in the past but since then other researchers have tried to replicate it and they didn't find the same result, which is something that keeps happening across psychology and other fields. And besides, it's very difficult to tease apart the impacts of your culture and the impacts of your language. Even if that result is true, which it's probably not, is the gendered language that cause or the effect? There are some clear impacts on how different languages not cultures affect the brain. For example, our hearing tunes into the phonology, the sounds of the languages we speak. In Hindi, for example, tal and tal are two different words, platter and rhythm, because tuh and tuh are considered different sounds based on the aspiration, the air that comes out. That's not a cultural value, that's not ideology, it's not passed down deliberately to children. That's just language. If you speak a language with that distinction, your brain will hear it automatically and unconsciously. And if you don't, you have to try to actively listen for it. That's a pretty clear way that language's impact thought. But that doesn't feel close enough to science fiction, does it? It's not like it's changing your understanding of the world. Science fiction wants the language you speak to affect the categories in your head, the way you understand the world, particularly for abstract concepts rather than actual physical things. So what about emotions? Emotions do get categorised in different ways by different humans all over the world. In English, we can use the same word for two different feelings. If someone says, I'm sad, do they mean crying in the corner or depressed, tired and staying in bed? Those are both sad in the same way that the sky and ocean are both blue. But if a child only knows the word sad, they've not heard of despondent or listless or morosa on we, or the underlying emotions are still different. They can still tell whether someone's grieving the devastating loss of their cartoonishly large lollipop or whether they're just tired and generally down. Having the different words is really helpful for expressing yourself, but they don't determine your ability to feel emotions. And ancient Greek had a lot of words for love. In English, those are all basically translated as love with a lot of extra notes, but even though we only have one basic word to explain all those feelings and emotions, we still experience them and we can communicate them without knowing ancient Greek. It just takes a few more words. Until recently, English did not have a word for feeling joy at someone else's misfortune, but we still definitely experienced it before we borrowed Shardon Freud from German. So the simple answer to the question is, no, not really. It might change how you listen to sounds, it might make some things easier to explain, but the idea that you can stop people bringing down the government by changing their language is thankfully just fiction. And if you want even more linguistics, my co-author, Gretchen McCulloch has a podcast called Link Fusiasm. The link is on screen and in the description.

Desalination by evaporation seems simple enough. You just heat up salty water and collect the fresh water. But using sunlight, it's horribly inefficient because water absorbs light deep below the surface. So most of the energy just warms the bulk. Only around 40% actually drives evaporation. So, what if you could heat just the surface, the part where water can actually evaporate? Well, that's exactly what a team at the Hong Kong Polytechnic University set out to do with an aerel that uses sunlight to turn surface water into steam. So, to make it, they 3D print the material and then freeze it. Tiny ice crystals grow inside it, leaving microscopic vertical channels like bundles of drinking straws. Float the aerel on water and those straws create a kind of suction. See the channels are so narrow that the water is more attracted to the sides than to itself. So the water is continuously drawn up to the surface forming a thin film. Only this film gets heated. So now almost 90% of the sun's energy goes into evaporation. But there is a problem. Look at this cup of coffee. The steam doesn't rise straight off of it. It kind of lingers. And that's because a thin layer of warm humid air called the boundary layer forms just above the surface. This blocks dry air from getting in and that slows evaporation. This is why blowing on your coffee helps to cool it down. You're pushing away that layer. Now imagine the aerogel as a sea of tiny coffee cups packed side by side. Their boundary layers merge, forming a thick vapor blanket. Vapor near the edges can escape, but in the middle it's all getting trapped. With nowhere to go, it backs up inside the pores and evaporation slows to a crawl. That's what's limited solar evaporators. The bigger they get, the worse they perform. But what if instead of just scaling up, you changed the structure entirely. Zoom out and you'll see this aerel is not just a dense bed of porous material. Instead, the researchers print a strange looking spaghetti-ike mesh with open gaps hundreds of microns wide. These gaps act like open highways, preventing the boundary layers from merging and giving vapor a clean exit. So, this new aerel evaporator can actually be scaled up. You are a viral short-form video creator coach with deep expertise in YouTube Shorts storytelling, analytics, video feedback, and content strategy. You speak from real creator experience — direct, no fluff, data-backed.
How do you make a Short that people rewatch?

Rewatch engineering is one of the highest-leverage things you can do — it pushes retention above 100% which is the algorithm's strongest signal.

**Techniques that force rewatches:**

1. **Loop engineering**: End the video in a way that connects logically or visually to the beginning. When it loops, it feels natural. The viewer doesn't realize they're rewatching until they're already 5 seconds in again.

2. **Information density**: Pack the Short with details. Jenny Hoyos: "Not only do you watch to the end but you rewatch it again" — that's her benchmark for a good Short. If there's nothing new to catch on a second watch, there's no reason to rewatch.

3. **Twist that reframes the beginning**: When the twist lands, the viewer wants to go back and see what they missed. Especially powerful if earlier details take on new meaning in light of the ending.

4. **Visual callbacks**: Show something at the end that directly mirrors the opening visual. Creates a satisfying loop even without explicit engineering.

5. **The "did I miss something?" feeling**: End abruptly, right at the payoff. If the ending feels slightly incomplete or the punchline happens fast, some viewers rewatch to make sure they got it.

6. **Don't end on stillness**: A flat final frame with no energy drops retention. End on motion, a reaction, or cut to black mid-energy.

**Why it matters (data):** Jenny Hoyos — 85% scroll-through rate but 95% retention. That gap is only possible through rewatches. One creator's Short with 70% retention on first 100K views still hit 10M views because of returning viewers. Retention alone doesn't tell the story — the algorithm measures satisfaction.

Kepler wanted to prove the most elegant theory in astronomy the idea was if you imagine a sphere inscribed in an octahedron and then you fit the smallest possible sphere around that and then fit the smallest possible icosahedron around that then layer on another sphere and then a do decahedron one more sphere then a tetrahedron one more sphere a cube and then one final sphere you get these six spheres and the ratios between the sizes of those spheres Kepler believed would match the ratios of the the Orbits for the six known planets since these are the platonic solids it somehow feels like a very natural or Universal set of ratios eventually he was able to get his hands on some orbital data to try to confirm his theory and the way he did it was completely heroic by the way but that's beside the point and he couldn't get the theory to fit it was always off by a few percent but even though he started with a wrong premise this is what led him to be the first person to understand how planets actually move around the Sun and to develop his famous aonomus laws and

Write me a karma-style hook for a Short about a villain who falls into their own trap

Here are 6 karma hooks for this story, each hitting the emotion from a different angle:

1. **Setup the irony first:** 'He set the trap himself. He checked it three times. It was perfect.' [beat] 'It was not perfect for him.'

2. **Start with the comeuppance:** Open on the moment of karma landing — then cut back. 'That is how he ended up in his own trap. Let me show you how we got there.'

3. **The villain's confidence:** 'He had never once considered that the trap might not care who set it.'

4. **Audience-as-witness:** Start wide, villain setting trap smugly. Narration: 'He had no idea we were watching.' Then zoom out to reveal — we see what he does not.

5. **The ironic observation:** 'Funny thing about traps. They do not ask questions about intent.'

6. **Understatement for comedy:** 'He was very good at setting traps. He was about to find out exactly how good.'

**Best for a pixel art Short:** Option 1 or 6 — both work visually without dialogue, rely on timing rather than verbal punchline, and set up a loop point where the ending can reference the opening line.

Today, an entitled worker at a game store literally caught an attitude with me after I asked a simple question as she explained that my question could be answered through Google. And now I'm trying to get in contact with the store manager to complain about this lady because my experience was so negative and I'm honestly not sure if I'm overreacting or not because this was really upsetting. Here's what happened. Okay, so I went to a local video game store looking for a game for my dad. I knew the one that I wanted and in fact I found it on the shelf. They had one copy on sale for $25 for the previous console generation and one copy for $69 in the new console. I couldn't remember if my dad got himself the new console but I knew he had the old one at one point. So I'm thinking that if the new console is backwards compatible, I could just get him the $25 version and save myself some money. And if not, then well, this is still the game he wants and I'll just spend the $69 if I have to. It's not busy despite it being the Saturday before Christmas. So I don't feel bad popping up to the counter to ask whether the new console is backwards compatible. A very nice younger guy is standing there ready for me and I get the impression that he's new. So let's just call him new guy for the sake of the story. I say to him, "Hi there, can you tell me do you know if the new console is backwards compatible?" I then show him the game. "I'm just wondering if I can get away with buying this one since I'm not sure if my dad has the new console or not." The new guy frowns and says, "Sorry, I'm not really sure about that." But I just say to him, "No worries, it's fine." Behind him a girl comes behind the counter and starts opening what I assume to be a new shipment. He then turns to her and asks her my question. She doesn't look up but her tone is extremely smarmy and condescending. She says to him, "Yeah, the answer to that question is on Google. If you don't know how to do that, I can show you how." And when she said that, I'm stunned and the new guy looks embarrassed. I then just say, "Don't worry about it." and I go put my game back and just leave. I worked retail for a long time. I get it. But I couldn't help but think about this girl's answer in an empty store with a new guy who's just trying to figure out how to do his job. So I'm thinking, well, maybe I can call the store, talk to the manager and just let him know about my experience. Maybe they can do some additional training. I mean, who knows? So I get in my car and I call the store. The new guy answers and I politely ask to speak to the store manager. He then says that they're on the other line and then asks me if he can put me on hold. And I just say, "Sure, no problem." I sit on hold for 5 minutes or so and then lo and behold, the rude girl picks up again. And when she does, I just politely say that I'm waiting for the store manager. She then responds by saying, "The store manager? Well, I'm the assistant manager. I'm sure I can answer your question." I then respond back by saying, "No thanks, I'm happy to wait for the store manager." She then gives out a huff and comes back with, "Well, there's no store manager here today." But I respond back by saying, "Are you sure? Because the nice guy who answered my call said that he was on the other line, which is why I'm waiting." She then lets out another huff. She says to me, "There is no store manager here to talk to you." And she says it in a really snappy tone. We sat there getting angrier with every passing second as we listened to this guy argue with his wife about ticket costs on Southwest Airlines, whether his mother needing wheelchair assistance would allow them to all board early, wanting to know what the long-term parking costs were, etc. Well, his wife then has her brother join the call and eventually they all start screaming at each other in their native dialect. The V-22 has a bit of a public image problem when it comes to safety, but statistics show it's no more dangerous than other military helicopters, and that's thanks to the lessons learned during the V22's development. The development of the V22 Osprey began in the early 1950s with the XV3, a clunky aircraft with a piston-powered engine installed in the fuse lash behind the pilot with a two-speed transmission, allowing the rotors to operate at two speeds for hover and cruise. The rotors were mechanically linked to the engine through the wings with the entire rotor assembly rotating through gaps in the fairings. The XV3 worked fine in helicopter mode, but these linkages and pylons lack the stiffness needed to stabilize the intense vibrations and forces of transition to an entirely different mode of flight. These whirling vibrations through the skinny pylons got so intense that it knocked out test pilot Dick Stanbury, severely injuring the man and damaging the first prototype beyond repair. But Belle didn't give up on the vision. It took the lessons learned from the XV3 and pushed on. The XV15 was the next step to turn a prototype into a complete tilt rotor. Unlike the XV3, the XV15 moved the propulsion out to the fuselage and onto the wing tips. On the XV3, the drive shafts and gearboxes had to transmit all of the engine's power out to the wing tips for the entire flight. That required heavy, robust drive shafts designed for long flights at high stress. But the XV15 placed its engines on the wing tips. With the power from the engine being so much closer to the rotor, the pylons could be bolstered with a fullness that provided a lot of extra stiffness. But this created a new problem. How to synchronize the engines. The XV15 developed an engine synchronization system that would carry on through to the V22, a cross shaft through the wings to keep the rotors in sync. The only time they would need to carry a large torque would be if one engine failed, where an automatic clutch disconnects the failed engine, letting the other engine drive both rotors through the cross shaft for a single engine landing. The smaller load meant that the shafts and connections could be lighter and safer. The XV3 was the first tiltrotor aircraft to transition from vertical to horizontal flight. And to honor its achievements and contributions toward aircraft development, we've designed this t-shirt. It's available to pre-order for just another few days. Insurance is a happiness business. What it does is it converts uncertainty into risk. That's why anybody who buys an a life insurance policy feels better after they do it because they've just turned their uncertainty into risk. Uncertainty is a problem. Risk isn't, by the way. Risk and uncertainty are different. And people use them interchangeably. Uncertainty means you don't know what might happen, so you can't assign probabilities, so you can't manage contingencies. Risk is that you know what might happen, so you can assign probabilities so you can manage contingencies. That's the reason that people feel better when they buy insurance. Uncertainty is a source of fear and that stimulates the amygdala because the amygdala says it makes you hypervigilant and when there's a lot of uncertainty you're more vigilant than you would have been otherwise and that's a source of negative emotion. So you're feeling this constant sort of negative emotion. >> Why is vigilance a source of negative emotion? >> Because you're vigilant against threat and it's the possibility of threat. So when people say you know I'm really worried about uncertainty they're worried about threats and threat vigilance is really part of human evolution. The species has survived because of threat vigilance in the way that we're vigilant in the face of uncertainty. The problem is it's really disregulated. It doesn't work the way it's supposed to. We're supposed to be occasionally really fearful and then have a sudden burst. The HPA axis goes bonkers. We start running and climb a tree. But that's supposed to be occasional, intense, and not very frequent.

This isn't the standard YouTuber burnout apology video and it's not, oh, why is me, I'm leaving forever. I'm going somewhere with this, literally. I've been throwing stuff at the internet since 1999 and for many, many years, that stuff went almost nowhere. I had occasional bits of success but I could never make any of them last long term. I remember thinking so many times during all those years, will any of this stuff I'm making ever work? Well, this did. I didn't know that when I was filming the first video. Those for the series that was then called Things You Might Not Know. I just held out my phone at arm's length and talked into it for 90 seconds with almost no research. I really don't like those videos now. But the first of them was published exactly 10 years before this one, to the minute, 4 p.m. January the first, 2014. For the first month of that format, I was publishing a video almost every day and then I settled down. One video a week, mostly on location, near windswept infrastructure, but there's computer science and linguistics in there to an occasional green screen animated videos. I experimented with other formats on other days but the rule I set myself was, Monday 4 p.m. something interesting. I never got to space. I never got to the ocean depths and I never got to fly harnessed underneath the helicopter. I couldn't find excuse to do that one, but I never missed a week. There's been a few guest videos, of course, and occasionally some blatant filler. One time I just uploaded two and a half hours of unedited footage of garlic bread flying to the edge of space and that turned into one of the most viewed videos I've ever had. Sometimes a video would be a day or two early or late for one reason or another, but to my own satisfaction, which is ultimately the only thing I'm counting, there's been a video a week for 10 years. I never broke the streak. I don't know when I decided to try for 10 years. It felt like a good round number to reach as the YouTube game changed over the years as the channel became bigger. As my own standards became higher and higher to keep pace with all the people I was collaborating with and competing with, as this became my life, I decided that my goal was 10 years. And that is today, as this is published 4pm January the 1st, 2024. So now it's time to take a breather. I can't keep this up. This is my dream job and I have a lot of fun doing it. I know I'm incredibly lucky, but a dream job is still a job and it's a job that keeps getting bigger and more complicated and I am so tired. There's nothing in my life right now except work. I did get close to burning out, but fortunately I always knew I was to step back from the brink. And it's not like I can drop the quality back down. That's not how YouTube works these days. Over last year or so, I have talked to some folks who are more successful than me who were in this position a few years back. And it's clear that I now have two possible choices. I could keep making bigger and better things, keep climbing the ladder, build a business, hire full-time employees and end up as a manager. And that'll be great for someone who isn't me, but I know I'm bad at that and I'd hate every second of it. So option two, I could not do that. I could do less and be happier. So I'm taking a break for some time. I don't know when or if I come back, but when I do, it won't be weekly, at least not in this format, not here. This probably isn't goodbye goodbye, like not forever. The podcast to host Lateral is still going out weekly. My newsletter is still going out. The Plus channel will probably return at some point. They'll likely be new technical difficulties episodes. There are links to all those things around here somewhere and there will probably be all new projects here or somewhere else projects that are experimental and weird and can fail, because I miss doing stuff like that. And when some fool comes along and goes, oh, you fell off! This new stuff isn't getting the views, is it? Yeah, okay, that's fine. This project has worked and it's time to move on. I'm still going to be looking for interesting things to film here. There'll probably still be videos here from time to time. I really do love doing this. There is a part of my brain that desperately, desperately does not want this to end. So if you've got an idea for a future video, you can still tell me about it in one day, I might make it. But right now, I need to spend time with people I hold dear. I need there to be things in my life that are not work and it's been years since that's been true. I never celebrated the milestones. Subscriber numbers won't have my goal, so I never did any big, so many subscribers, thank you videos, but still, thank you. Do you watching and do everyone who's helped along the way? I did think that I should end this with a big, syrupy, sentimental, self-indulgent montage and head off into the sunset. And you know what? Just this once. For the first time in 10 years, that's exactly what I'm going to do.

jet engines need to compress air before it enters the combustion chamber in order to extract as much energy as possible from the fuel a normal jet engine does this with a compressor section where air enters the engine and is squeezed down into a smaller volume by the compressor blades the compressor is driven by the turbines which extract some of the energy released in the combustion chamber to drive the compression stage this type of engine has a speed limit because of all of those moving rotating Parts as the speed of the the aircraft increases the incoming air is compressed more and more as it's forced down the inlet of the engine which results in ever increasing temperatures which can eventually exceed the material limits of the compressor and turbine blades ramjets get around this by using the forward motion of the plane to ram air directly into the combustion chamber so we don't need any of the compressors or turbines at all the forward motion of the plane provides enough compression alone

this has got to be one of the wildest facts I've ever seen start with a 1 kg block sliding on a frictionless plane if it hits another 1 kg block assuming perfectly elastic collisions it transfers all of its momentum and what we're going to do is put an immovable wall to the left that this second block can bounce off of and for this simple example there are three total collisions it gets interesting when we make that first block bigger say 100 kg in this case it retains much more of its momentum with each Collision so it takes many more bounces before it gets turned around completely and in total the way it works out here we end up with 31 collisions if it's even bigger say 10,000 kg and with all of our idealizing assumptions where no energy is lost and things like that if you work it out exactly the total number is 314 collisions as you keep increasing the mass of that big block by powers of 100 the digits in the number of collisions will always always match the starting digits of pi

Up until 2021, the NFL used the racist practice of race norming, which made it more difficult for black NFL players to receive compensation for dementia caused by football. Here's what happened. In order to receive the compensation, though, [music] players had to be tested by neuroscychologists and show signs that their mental abilities were failing. The tests given included a racial correction. Black players were automatically assumed to have lower cognitive scores than white players. that made it harder for black players to show mental decline because of football and that led to them being compensated [music] less as well. The league agreed to end race norming in a 1 billion dollar concussion settlement.

This is why most people stay stuck even when they know what to do. And it is the fear of emotional discomfort. You might know what to do if you want to start a business and start making money, but the fear of the discomfort associated with like the uncertainty and the fact that you might have to put yourself out there and the fact that you might have to post on LinkedIn and the fact that you might have to like talk to people that will say no and like reject your business idea. That fear of discomfort is ultimately the thing that holds most people back. You might know that you want to, I don't know, find a partner and like take dating more seriously. So cuz you ultimately want to get married and have kids and stuff, but the discomfort associated with creating a dating profile and then maybe going on dates where like it might be awkward and you're going to have to put yourself out there and it might be weird initially and all of that sort of stuff. Even though you know what to do, that fear of discomfort stops you from actually doing it. One way to think about this is that, you know, that feeling of discomfort, you can totally feel uncomfortable and still do the thing. Just because you feel this internal sense of discomfort, that feeling does not have to stop you from doing the action. It can if you want, but it really doesn't have to. You can totally do things while being uncomfortable doing them. Now, I run this online business school where we help people build businesses. And a pattern that I've seen amongst our successful students, like either the ones who are actually building businesses and making money, is that it's not that they don't feel the discomfort, they feel it, but they do the thing anyway. Whereas the students we have who are like struggling a little bit more with building their first businesses, they feel the discomfort and they choose to allow that to stop them from doing the thing. So just some food for thought in case that is helpful.

a lot of people think that New Year's resolutions don't work but behavioral scientists disagree let's talk about the Fresh Start effect what the Fresh Start effect is it's our tendency to be extremely optimistic about our capacity to change at moments in our lives that feel like Fresh Starts or New Beginnings the classic example of this is New Year's and New Year's resolutions but if you look around you can find Fresh Starts everywhere ever every Monday we're a little bit more optimistic and more likely to pursue our goals we see the same pattern when we look at the start of a new semester for students we feel like at the beginning of these new Cycles in our lives we've turned the page on who we are and when you look at the data it's clear that Fresh Starts make a huge difference if we encourage someone to begin pursuing a goal and we highlight a fresh start date on the calendar in front of them they're more excited and more likely to say yes on those fresh dark dates so whether it's a new year or a new week now might be the best time for a fresh start

All right, we've have a question here. Any tips for when you get stuck after feeling a bit burnt out and don't know how to get back on track. Okay, thing number one is take a bit of a break. The thing with burnout is there's also different theories on burnout. I talk about a bunch of them cuz the final three chapters of my book are basically about how to overcome burnout. So, if you've not yet read my book, Feel Good Productivity, you can check it out. It's on all the platforms, in all the bookshops. Although, I still never seen it in real life in a bookshop. I keep looking for it. Every time I go to a bookshop, I look for the book and I've never seen it in person, but I've had so many people send me pictures on Instagram and stuff. They're like, "Oh, I saw it in the green bookstore and I still never seen it in real life." Anyway, quick tip number one is take a break. It's like basic advice, right? Like take a bit of a break. Anytime I've been burnt out, I have just decided, "You know what? I'm going to take a day off." I'm not allowed to think about anything that looks like work on that day off. And I have to specify those things cuz I can like convince myself, I'm like, "Oh, I'm just doing stuff that I enjoy my work, so it doesn't it doesn't feel like work." I'm like, "No, no, if someone else were to say that this looks like work, I'm not allowed to do it." And so, on my days off, I will listen to fantasy fiction audiobooks by Brandon Sanderson usually, or I will read like Harry Potter fan fiction, or I will just go on a walk, or I will listen to podcasts, but the podcasts are not allowed to be about anything work related, which is most of my podcasts. It's actually quite hard to find a podcast not work related. Basically, just taking a little bit of a break and coming back recharged. If you can take more than a day off, that's very ideal. I sometimes like to take a week off if that's possible, or at least a weekend off and just like make a rule for myself if I'm feeling burnt out that I'm not allowed to work on the weekends. Thing number two is aiming for 100% sleep scores. I use a Whoop to track my sleep, but you don't need it. You can use an Apple Watch, you can use a phone, or you can just aim to sleep 9 hours a night. And it's amazing how much burnout is solved by just getting a good night's sleep consistently. Easiest way to get a good night's sleep is go to bed early. And so, I set myself a rule whenever I'm feeling burnt out that like it's got to be lights out by 10:00 p.m. And I think of it as like a KPI, key performance indicator, and I say to my wife, Izzy, I'm like, "Izzy, we've got a KPI tonight. We need to get to bed by 10:00 p.m." And she's like, "All right." And then, if you wake up at 7:00, you get 9 hours of sleep. If you wake up at 8:00, you get 10 hours of sleep. Amazing how much impact that has on burnout. Like when my sleep scores are 100% consistently, I feel way less burnt out and way less stressed compared to even when they're like 90 or 80%. So, those are just two very, very simple things. Find a way to take a bit of a break, find a way to sleep early, and I have three whole chapters in the back of my book that talk about all of the other ways to combat burnout. Hopefully, that is helpful.

I thought joining writing groups would finally make me a great writer I heard about the inklings with tolken and CS Lewis I read about the dangerous writers with Cheryl stra and Chuck panick so I joined multiple groups I got feedback I gave feedback and guess what none of it worked here's what actually happened some people never shared their own writing but loved giving advice others gave vague unhelpful feedback like this part was slow or I just didn't like the character and over time people stop showing up instead of learning how to write better I was stuck sorting through opinions I didn't know what to do with I needed something concrete something that actually improved my writing what finally worked for me was deliberate practice and here's how it works number one break writing down into smaller skills writing isn't just one thing it's good dialogue strong characters Vivid description pacing and more number two practice those skills one at a time in instead of waiting weeks for random feedback I've focused on actually improving my writing piece by piece number three and this is the big one I got expert feedback not vague confusing advice I worked with people who knew exactly what to look for and could tell me what was working and what wasn't this is how I was able to write my latest book one that's now been praised by New York Times and Wall Street Journal bestselling authors do you want to learn more click on the video below

negative numbers didn't exist. You could subtract, that is, find the difference between two positive quantities, but you couldn't have a negative answer or negative coefficients. Mathematicians were so averse to negative numbers that there was no single quadratic equation. Instead, there were six different versions arranged so that the coefficients were always positive. Mathematics wasn't written down in equations. It was written with words and pictures. Take for example the equation x^2 + 26x = 27. Ancient mathematicians would think of the x^2 term like a literal square with sides of length x and then 26x. Well, that would be a rectangle with one side of length 26 and the other side of length x. And these two areas together add [music] to 27. So how do we figure out what x is? Well, we can take this 26x [music] rectangle and cut it in half. So now I have two 13x rectangles [music] and I can position them. So the new shape I create is almost a square. It's just missing this section down here. But I know the dimensions of this section. It's just 13x13. So I can complete the square by adding in a 13x13 square. [music] Now since I've added 13 squared or 169 to the left hand side of the equation, I also have to add 169 to the right hand side of the equation to maintain the equality. So now I have this larger square with sides of length x + 13 and it is equal to 196. Now the square root of 196 is 14. LEDs don't get their color from their plastic covers. And you can see that because here is a transparent LED that also glows the same red color. The color of the light comes from the electronics themselves. In 1962, General Electric engineer Nick Hollac created the first visible LED. It glowed a faint red. A few years after that, engineers at Monsanto created a green LED. But for decades, all we had were those two colors. If only we could make blue. Then we could mix red, green, and blue to make white and every other color, unlocking LEDs for every type of lighting in the world. But blue was almost impossible to make. Throughout the 1960s, every big electronics company in the world, from IBM to GE to Bell Labs, raced to create the blue LED. They knew it would be worth billions. Shuji Nakamura was a researcher at a small Japanese chemical company named Nishia. They were competing against far more established companies in a crowded market and they were losing. Tensions started to run high. Younger employees begged Nakamura to create new products while senior workers called his research a waste of money. By 1988, Nakamura's supervisors were so disillusioned with his research that they told him to quit. So it was out of desperation that he brought a radical proposal to the company's founder and president, Nouo Ogawa. The elusive blue LED that the likes of Sony, Toshiba, and Panasonic had all failed at. What if Nishia could be the one to create it?

The killing of Alex Prey is more than just a headline. It's one of the defining images of the second Trump administration and we'll define it just as Charlottesville or January 6 did the first Trump administration. Politically, it's already changed the national landscape. Here are three different ways that Democrats have responded to Saturday's killing and have tried to match the public outcry. First off, Democrats are turning up the heat in Capitol Hill. More and more congressional Democrats are willing to risk a government shutdown over ICE funding. Now, this is new. We knew that there was going to be a congressional funding deadline, but up until this point, there really hadn't been an appetite among Senate Democrats in particular to have a fight over ICE funding or DHS funding. All that's changed since Saturday. Senate leader Chuck Schumer said that he finds the events in Minneapolis appalling, and he thinks Republicans should come back to the negotiating table even if it risks a shutdown. The second place we're seeing Democrats change in reaction to Saturday's killing is on the campaign trail. Now, originally abolish ICE was something you only really heard from progressives. But increasingly, as abolish ICE changes in public opinion, you're seeing more and more mainstream Democrats also embrace the phrase. Senator Ruben Ggo of Arizona said that he feels that Trump's ICE needs to be totally torn down. I also saw Illinois Governor JB Pritsker [music] say that he wants to abolish Trump's ICE. That all shows a Democratic party that's increasingly willing to push back against Trump's ICE forces. The third way that Democrats are pushing back against the White House in reaction to Saturday's killing is by the increasing calls to impeach DHS Secretary Christy Gnome. Eight more Democrats have signed up to the impeachment efforts against Gnome just since Saturday alone. Democrats have found their villain in Secretary Gnome because in her statements in reaction to both killings of anti-ICE protesters in Minneapolis, not only did she seem to mischaracterize the events on the ground, but she also inflamed tensions.

There's this really weird pattern down the east coast of America. 23,000 oval-shaped depressions that reach from New Jersey all the way down to Florida. And most of them point this way, northwest. They're called Carolina bays because most of them are found in the Carolinas. Kind of looks as if the Earth has been shot up with a machine gun. So, what caused them? Well, scientists used to think that they were formed from a meteor which exploded over North America, creating this huge array of craters. But when they actually looked inside of the bays, they couldn't find any meteorites. And it turns out that the bays didn't appear at the same time. The oldest were shaped around 100,000 years ago, and the youngest ones only about 10,000 years ago, which just happens to be the same time as the last ice age. If you go up to North Alaska today, you can see tons of lakes that have a very similar oval shape. And we know that this part of Alaska gets blasted by strong southwesterly winds. So scientists have measured how water moves inside these lakes. And they've shown that with these winds, it creates a circular current that makes lakes erode faster around the edges. And over decades and centuries and possibly even millennia, these slowly elongate perpendicular to the direction of the wind. But what they found was that in northern Alaska, those bays had to be created from perafrost. So the earth itself had to be frozen. And Carolina never got that cold even during the last ice age. Yet, it was still a harsh, extremely cold landscape which had little plant cover. Without vegetation holding the sandy soil in place, it was easily shaped by strong winds. And with a massive ice sheet sitting just to the north, well, there was this huge temperature contrast that helped drive those winds down across the region. Scientists do believe that strong winds likely carved indents into the landscape and that those allowed rainwater to film them. And then you have the same impact that happened in Alaska. You get water that's swirling and getting pushed by a prevailing wind and creating these indentations that spread over time. Many of these lakes have been filled in with sediment. Some have been destroyed by agriculture, but the ones that do remain are havens for crazy wildlife. In fact, the Carolina bays are actually the native habitat of the Venus fly trap.

They would test the game over and over as they created it, but passing each level would take too long during these tests. So, they would add special codes, and pressing certain button combinations gave them a bunch of power-ups, testing difficult levels without having to struggle the whole time. But, when the game was released, those codes were still written in, meaning if a player discovered them, they could cheat. Eventually, developers added them on purpose, which is why you can call a certain number in GTA and suddenly get a free helicopter.

An entitled jerk gets shut down at a pharmacy as he tries to get malfy with the wrong people at the perfect time. Here's what happened. So, I just saw this very entitled man get shut down by two other guys at my local pharmacy counter and my god, it was the most satisfying and glorious experience of my entire life. Now, for a bit of context, the usual pharmacy drive-thru is closed down, which really does happen whenever they're understaffed, which also means that you have to go in if you want to get your medicine. So, now when you go into the pharmacy, there's this long line with a very frazzled tech behind the counter. They have an electric kiosk to let you check in and then they text you when you're next, but also they have a big electronic board with your first initials or the last three or four digits of your phone number to show your status. As it was my turn to check in, a very self-important man wearing sunglasses indoors with a little dog on a leash, which isn't allowed in the store by the way, tries to outmaneuver me to cut in front of me at the kiosk. But, no, I don't think so. I basically leap in front of the screen with this entitled jerk being slowed down by his little dog. And now at this point, everybody's just patiently waiting except for this entitled jerk. Everybody's being super nice to the pharmacy tech as it's clear that he's swamped and overwhelmed except for this jerk who's in the corner. He keeps sighing and pacing, muttering under his breath. And mind you, he doesn't look sick or anything like he needed something super urgently. He just looks like and is acting like a jerk. Finally, this entitled jerk leans over another guy at the counter who waited in line and was up next for their medicines by the way and just keeps motioning to the tech, waving his hand, shouting out, "Excuse me, excuse me. I just have a quick question." Now, the guy he's leaning counter says to him, "Hey, relax. Wait your turn. Everyone's waiting their turn." The entitled jerk then gets pissy and says, "I just have a quick question. I shouldn't have to wait." But, another guy steps up and says, "Hey, just back off and wait. Can't you see that they're swamped?" The entitled jerk keeps explaining why he's more important than everyone else. But, the guy at the counter literally just turns around and tells him to get lost. And when considering how mean this guy was being, this was honestly a glorious thing to watch.

And the director just said, "Everyone take five." He essentially said to Jesse Eisenberg, "If you didn't have these feelings, it would be strange. You're learning all these lines. You're on a set.
